Figure 19: Visualisation of images inpainted by various methods, including LAMA [35], MAT [25] and ZIT [13]. Red areas indicate untextured areas caused by occlusion of furniture and inpainted using the inpainting methods.

Figure 2: Method overview: Our system constructs detailed textured 3D room models through a two-phase pipeline. Initially, the Capturing step collects a calibrated set of RGB-D images and creates the original mesh (Moriginal). In the Mesh Processing step, this mesh is filtered using the room layout to produce a filtered mesh (Mfiltered), which is then integrated with the layout to create the combined mesh (Mcombined). A new set of RGB images is captured in the Texture Image Acquisition step, which is utilized in the second phase’s Texturing step. Here, the Mcombined and these RGB images are used to apply textures to the mesh. Following this, the Plane2Image step generates a set of images corresponding to the room’s walls. These images are then refined in the Post-Texturing step, which finalizes the pipeline.
Figure 24: Additional visualizations of the textured walls for each room after the inpainting process - 1/2.

Figure 18: Examples of the Post Texturing (ImageMode) results using the stable diffusion model (PowerPaint [48]), which is a serverbased service on RoomRecon. In the first example (the first row), we input the text ”A painting, hanging on the wall, portrays a city at night in the style of Van Gogh”. In the second example (the second row), the input text is ”a flying balloon”.
Figure 17: Examples of plane images before and after inpainting: We use ZIT [13] as our inpainting model. Red masks are untextured areas caused by occlusion by furniture.

Figure 8: Qualitative results of textured planes using PlaneOpt [38], SeamlessTex [15], Our-SeamlessTex, and Our-SeamlessTex after inpainting operation. For more visualizations of results using Ours-Inpainted, refer to the supplementary material (Section A.7).

Figure 9: Textured room layouts after completing our RoomRecon pipeline. Samples with dashed lines show ceiling samples used for creating ceiling textures and samples in solid lines show floor samples for floor textures. For additional viewpoints of the textured layouts, refer to the supplementary material (Section A.7).
Figure 3: Processes and outputs of the Mesh Processing step.
Figure 25: Additional visualizations of the textured walls for each room after the inpainting process - 2/2.

Figure 23: Visualization of textured rooms: The left column shows the room layouts, while the 2nd and 3rd columns show textured room layouts seen from two different perspectives. The 4th and 5th columns show samples used for the generation of floor and ceiling textures.
